messages.content:
**P1 — No**

He flubbed saying don't.

"Don't k- they don't get any kills in this run, they don't get a bomb plant."

Donk was not even playing.

Here is the disputed mention:

https://www.youtube.com/live/v0-0BDYKLUg?si=XzUDQ_rpXq9qvuNN&t=19355

https://imgur.com/IhqHyR5

It keeps being said by P2 arguers that the o sounds like donkey. I have no idea what they are talking about. The o sound of the disputed mention is like in "dome" or "don't." Not "donkey." So all the clips from Laccs actually help the P1 argument.

Here's one isolated. This is how Donk, the player, is said:
https://www.youtube.com/live/5dwJq533WXM?t=17099

Counting this would be like counting a mention of Trump talking about Scott Kupor in a rally and then something surprising happening in the crowd and saying "Scott Ku- wait!" and counting it as a mention of Kuwait.

I also don't think a mention of "I guess D- I guess Donald Trump wasn't bluffing" should count as a mention of "guessed."

One last P2 claim is that there is no t sound for don't. I don't think it even matters, but even if it does, I think they're incorrect. I hear it, though contracted into the k sound a bit. This is common with consonants that run together, *especially* with "t"s. You can replicate this yourself by saying "first light" really quickly. The t and l will contract together.

messages.content:
P1 - No

I was originally on the side of Yes because I thought anything that sounded like a "donk" should and equals a "donk". It was a simple argument, until I realized the flaw.

https://www.youtube.com/live/v0-0BDYKLUg?t=19355s

Let's establish that there are many ways to pronounce "don't" in a way such that "don't" sounds exactly like "donk". 

**If we say its Yes donk**, with the premise being ***we don't care about context***, then there would be at least three instances of  "**Donk**- they **donk **get any kills in this run, they **donk** get a bomb plant."

**There is either 3 mentions of donk or none at all!**

Of course context matters!

He flubbed saying don't.

"Don't k- they don't get any kills in this run, they don't get a bomb plant."

This is the simplest explanation using Occam's Razor. There is 0 reason to expect that he said **"Donk"** in the middle of nowhere.

The **intent **for this mention is about the famous CS player name **Donk**. The context of the accused mention doesn't make sense as he would refer to the player in the middle of nowhere.

Its time to stop the scam.

messages.content:
P2 - Yes

Even if announcer stuttered / weird context, market is about if “donk” was said (mention in videos listed above)

**P2 – Yes**

Clear and audible mention of 'Donk'. It wasn't his intention based on the context, however the word was pronounced clearly.

**P2 - Yes**

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v0-0BDYKLUg&t=19356s

One only needs to listen to the clip themselves.

**Does Context Matter?**

From the rules, "any usage of the term, regardless of context, will count toward the resolution of this market."

And to address an earlier (and incorrect) precedent argument, the Khamenei precedent literally establishes that context does NOT matter (Trump intended to say Khamenei but the market still went no), and hence context does NOT apply here.

**Was Donk Pronounced Correctly?**

An earlier argument argues that this is not the correct pronunciation of Donk, the player. This is false: below you can find many instances where Donk is pronounced the same as how the caster said it in the qualifying clip:

https://www.youtube.com/shorts/OqJpSBRHSQg

**Can you audibly hear "Don't Get" instead of "Donk"?**

Again, please listen to the qualifying clip. To those who say that "Don't Get" is quickly mispronounced of "Donk", try mispronuncing "Don't Get" yourself - I find it hard to believe that you can get what the caster said, without going out of your way to audibly say "Donk", which the caster ultimately did.

**Ultimately:**

1) context does not matter

2) Donk is said and pronounced in a qualifying manner

P2

"Donk" was audibly said. 

The only argument against this fact is that it was not intentional, which is irrelevant. There's a strong precedence for unintentional mentions and even mispronunciations and words not completed counting so this case were the word is clearly said in full must go Y to uphold precedence.

Another example of words counting despite being unintentional is "Inflation", which was settled as Y even despite the word not being completed before being corrected to unemployment.

P2 - Yes

Donk with K at the end was said.

Our [voting committees](<https://github.com/UMA-rocks/voting-committees>) are currently reviewing this dispute, we will edit this comment once they have made their final decision.

_Disclaimer: This post is for informational purposes only. We encourage all voters to do their own due diligence and to vote independently based on what they believe to be correct._

The rules say it themselves: "Any usage of the term, regardless of context, will count toward the resolution of this market." 

375 individual voters heard the clip in Round 1 and voted YES. 247 voted NO. The majority of human beings who actually listened said YES.
So why didn't it resolve YES? Because the NO side has more tokens. That's the entire reason.

Now I need every UMA holder to read what is happening in this thread, because the future of this protocol is being decided right now, not by the evidence, but by fear.
----
A Verifier in this thread wrote and I quote — "While I personally hear the 'donk'" and then voted NO. Why? Because the larger stake was on NO and they were afraid of getting slashed.

Another participant wrote that "P2 votes in subsequent rounds carry significant risk of slashes." That's not an argument about audio evidence. That's a threat.

A third said to "vote with the majority" not with the majority of voters, who voted YES — but the majority of tokens.

Read those again. These are people telling you, in writing, that they believe the answer is YES but they are voting NO because a whale is on the other side.

This is the moment UMA either proves it works or proves it doesn't.
----
UMA's entire value proposition — the reason any of your tokens are worth anything — is that the oracle resolves on **truth**. That's the promise. That's **the Schelling point.** __That's what makes UMA worth more than zero.__

If this dispute resolves NO: Not because NO is correct, but because one large holder has enough weight to scare rational voters away from truth — then what is UMA? It's not an oracle. It's an auction. Whoever holds the most tokens decides reality, and everyone else falls in line out of self-preservation.n

And if that's what UMA is, ask yourself: why would Polymarket keep using it? Why would any protocol trust an oracle where evidence doesn't matter and capital decides truth? They won't. They'll build something else or move to a competitor. And when they do, the value of every UMA token you hold goes to zero.

This is not about one esports market. This is about whether **UMA's Schelling point is truth or money.** And right now, in this thread, you can see it breaking in real time — people publicly stating YES and voting NO out of **fear.**
That is textbook oracle corruption.

P2 - YES

The resolution criteria are straightforward and should be applied as written.

The rules explicitly state that any usage of the term counts, regardless of context. This removes the need to interpret intent, meaning, or surrounding sentence structure. The only relevant question is whether the word “Donk” was audibly said.

In the available clip, the pronunciation includes a clear terminal “k” sound. While some may interpret the phrase differently in context, the rule explicitly eliminates context as a factor. This is therefore not a question of meaning, but of phonetic output.

Importantly, this is not a case of missing evidence — the clip exists and has been reviewed by all participants. The disagreement is not about availability of proof, but about interpretation of what is heard. In such cases, resolution should follow the rule’s simplest and most literal application.

A significant portion of voters (by count) independently identified the word as “Donk,” which further supports that this is not an isolated or unreasonable interpretation.

Choosing “No” requires introducing additional assumptions:
1)that intent overrides pronunciation
2)that context should be used despite rules stating otherwise
3)that an audible “k” can be dismissed

This adds unnecessary complexity and deviates from the rule’s clear wording.

Conclusion:
Since the rule counts any usage regardless of context, and the clip contains a defensible and audible instance of “Donk,” this market should resolve P2 – Yes.

P2 - Yes

Donk was said clearly in the clip can't really argue anything else

P2 - YES

P1 N

P2 - YES

You can clearly hear the 'k' sound — that is unambiguously the word 'donk' regardless of the speaker's intent. The market rule is straightforward: **as long as the pronunciation contains that word, it resolves Yes.**

https://discord.com/channels/718590743446290492/1492653069429641412/1493942355869306910 There is a very compelling spectrographic analysis supporting this.

Based on the UMA voter count, YES has **more than half the votes (376 > 246)**, which shows that the community is clearly pushing back against big voters trying to control the resolution.

**I think Polymarket should resolve based on the rules and the facts, rather than based on the price being driven up by whales.**
Vote p2

P2 - Yes

Since the "No" side is choosing to **FRAUDULENTLY** vote against the physical evidence, we shall let the actual data prevail.

> Obviously... final-obstruent devoicing... into a /k/

The symbol [ɡ̊] represents a "voiceless velar plosive," which is identical to a [k]. By arguing that the sound "devoiced,"  you admit that his vocal cords stopped vibrating and a **voiceless velar stop [k] was produced.** Whether you call it a "devoiced g" or a "k," the physical result is the same: **the [k] sound.**
This argument also claims the caster uttered the diphthong /oʊ/ (as in "don't"). This is **physically impossible**.
The caster is [Northern Irish](https://liquipedia.net/counterstrike/Dinko).  In Ulster English, this vowel is typically a steady monophthong. My Praat analysis shows a *steady-state monophthong.* This matches the expected phonetic realization of "Donk" in the speaker's native dialect perfectly. To claim he said "don't" is to ignore the specific linguistic constraints of Northern Irish phonology, which would require a completely different formant signature than what was originally recorded.

**"Donk" was said in isolation with no subject before it. The "They" only came later in the correction. The claim that it was a slurred "don't" has no ground when there was no sentence structure to slur it into.**

> reasonably concluded... intention was “Don’t”
If the claim is that the resolution should be "No" because the commentator "intended" to say don't, then **YOU ARE VOTING AGAINST THE CLEAR MARKET RULES.** Read the criteria: **"Regardless of context."** The commentator's thoughts, his stutter, and his self-correction are IRRELEVANT to the contract. Voting "No" because you have collectively decided the speaker's intent matters more than what was actually said is a bad-faith interpretation of the Polymarket rules.

> no reason to vote against the previous round majority unless you want to get slashed
This is not even a factual argument; it's just a threat. They openly admit that the only reason to vote with them is to avoid being slashed, they are admitting the facts are not on their side. Using fear to enforce a "No" vote is a desperate attempt to manipulate the Schelling point and create a false precedent.

> Partial/incomplete pronunciation doesn’t count... The announcer said don’t without the t and mixed with whatever next word. 
The spectrogram shows a sharp voiceless velar release burst. That is not a "missing t"... it is a **present k!** Furthermore, /n/ naturally becomes the velar nasal [ŋ] before a [k]. Differentiating "Dongk" from "Donk" is an outright rejection of basic linguistics. **This is a fully articulated realization of the word.**

Even the top "No" holder, aenews, states
> it sounds like donk to me, but it don't count as donk for the market.
This is a confession that the "No" side is not voting on what actually happened, but on a secondary narrative they've invented to protect their positions. If a sound "sounds like Donk," and a spectrogram proves it is "Donk," and the rules say "Regardless of context," then it MUST count for the market. To vote "No" while admitting the sound exists is a **direct attack on the integrity** of the UMA oracle. It is the definition of a "bad faith" resolution.

If it's a "dumb market," **why** is he so desperate to defend a position that contradicts the spectrographic physics? You don't get to call a market a waste of time while simultaneously gatekeeping the truth. Ultimately, the "No" side is trying to rewrite the contract mid-vote. The rules clearly state **"Regardless of context,"** which is specifically designed to prevent people from arguing about what a speaker "meant" to say.

**Vote on reality. Don't vote to support a SCAM because you didn't care to look at the facts, when they are clearly laid out via image, video, and audio. UMA is a truth machine; it is time that we treat it like one.**

**P2 - Yes**

**In theory, this market should be incredibly simple to resolve. **All one has to ask themselves is, “Did I hear the speaker say Donk?”, and vote alongside that belief.

**Reality could not be any different.** A few actors, some with tremendous influence, have pushed forth narratives which deviate from what the overwhelming number of us hear. If one looks at the first round of voting, they will see that 128 more voters committed to P2 than P1.

The P1 (NO) arguments are many, often duplicitous, and grow in volume each day. Most can be addressed by listening to the clip oneself. **But some P1 arguments betray a belief of superiority over the truth.** To be the minority that earnestly did not hear Donk is one thing. To tell us that context matters, abandoning market rules, or to tell us that speaker said something fundamentally different from what we ourselves can hear is another.

**Perhaps the most egregious narrative that a few P1 have spread is that, if you vote against them, you will be slashed.** This argument stands against the values of prediction markets and should speak for itself.

And yet, somehow, I doubt this narrative. If 375 voters are willing to stand up for the truth, against the few who flaunt their influence and wallet sizes, perhaps more can break away and vote for what they heard, when given the chance in this new round.

**To the voters, I say this:** while I believe that we can win, to what risk is it getting slashed? 0.1% of your tokens is not some financial hostage (especially when those tokens are down 59% in the past year). Many of you bought these tokens because you believed in the idea behind UMA, and perhaps still believe, but this idea is being tested at this very moment.

**At the end of the day, is it really that unlikely that many of us hear Donk? **Is it a far cry belief that one of the most notable CS2 casters inadvertently said the name of one of the most famous CS2 players in one of the biggest tournaments of the year, even if he did not intend to?

If this market can be resolved against a reality anyone can experience by watching a three second clip, than any market can be resolved against reality. **If you hear Donk, please vote for Donk.**

 Excited to see how this one turns out : - ).
